coredata.dc:title: Waste candle soot derived nitrogen doped carbon dots based fluorescent sensor probe: An efficient and inexpensive route to determine Hg(II) and Fe(III) from water
coredata.dc:creator: Pankaj, Aditya
Tewari, Kshitij
Singh, Shiv
Singh, Sheelendra Pratap
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an easy and facile route is followed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from candle soot (CS) for efficient and selective detection of mercury (Hg (II)) and iron (Fe (III)) toxic metals ions. These CDs were obtained via oxidation process are furthermore doped with nitrogen (N) to make them more competent towards the target applications. N-doped CDs (N-CDs) are synthesized by ethylene diamine (N source) and sodium lauryl sulphate (favorable for synthesis of smaller N-CDs). The average size of synthesized N-CDs is found in between 2.0 nm to 5.0 nm and they efficaciously have emitted blue FL under UV lamp exposure (∼290-365 nm). Zeta potential shows the net surface charge of N-CDs is negative (−10.4 mV) and plays an important role for the sensitivity of sensor and its binding efficiency towards metal ions. Functional groups (amine, hydroxyl, carbonyl, nitryl and alkenes) present on N-CDs are favorable for quenching and binding probability. Optimum pH was found 4–5 for getting better FL intensity of N-CDs. This FL is disappeared during quenching of certain concentrations of Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions due to the selective quenching of N-CDs with these metal ions. The linear range and optical detection limit of Hg (II) ions are 20–50 μM and 50 nM respectively, while for Fe (III) ions these values are 20–50 μM and 10.0 nM respectively. The fabricated N-CDs are inexpensive colorimetriC–Optical detector, selective and sensitive towards the detection of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions contaminations and could be used as FL sensor probe for the detection of toxic metals sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Candle soot
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Toxic heavy metal ions
Sensor probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity study with different metal ions
Sensitivity and linearity of Hg (II) and Fe (III)
Effect of pH and solvent with N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comprehensive schematic representation of fabrication and application of N-CDs from CS.
TEM images of prepared N-CDs at (a) low resolution (30 KX) (b) high resolution (52 KX), (c) XRD spectrum, (d) Raman spectra and (e) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs.
Full scan XPS spectra (a), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (b), high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (c) and high- resolution XPS spectra of O1s (d) of prepared N-CDs.

UV–vis spectra of N-CDs, and inset shows FL activity of N-CDs in UV region light (265–360 nm wavelength) (a); Excitation and emission (Excitation-344 nm, Emission-416 nm) (b) spectra of N-CDs and Selectivity study of Prepared N-CDs with different metal ions at 344 nm wavelength (c).
FL response of N-CDs in presence of Hg (II) (a) and Fe (III) (b). Inset shows error bar while plotting linearity range of respective metal ions.
Comparison table of optical parameters of prepared N-CDs.

row.entry: N-doped carbon dots derived from carbon soot
Fluorophore and functionally diverse co-linkers into Zn-based structures
Chitosan based carbon dots rooted agarose hydrogel film
N-S co-doped one-pot synthesis using caffeine
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots from casein
N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid
Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) using citric acid as the carbon source
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with the use of Chinese yams

head.ce:title: Waste candle soot derived nitrogen doped carbon dots based fluorescent sensor probe: An efficient and inexpensive route to determine Hg(II) and Fe(III) from water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pesticide Toxicology Laboratory & Regulatory Toxicology Group, CSIR-Indian Institute of Toxicology Research (CSIR-IITR), Vishvigyan Bhawan, 31 Mahatma Gandhi Marg, Lucknow, 226001, Uttar Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pesticide Toxicology Laboratory & Regulatory Toxicology Group

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterial Toxicology Group, CSIR-Indian Institute of Toxicology Research (CSIR-IITR), Vishvigyan Bhawan, 31 Mahatma Gandhi Marg, Lucknow, 226001, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Laboratory, CSIR-Indian Institute of Toxicology Research (CSIR-IITR), Vishvigyan Bhawan, 31 Mahatma Gandhi Marg, Lucknow, 226001, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: CSIR-Indian Institute of Toxicology Research (CSIR-IITR), Vishvigyan Bhawan, 31 Mahatma Gandhi Marg, Lucknow, 226001, Uttar Pradesh, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an easy and facile route is followed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from candle soot (CS) for efficient and selective detection of mercury (Hg (II)) and iron (Fe (III)) toxic metals ions. These CDs were obtained via oxidation process are furthermore doped with nitrogen (N) to make them more competent towards the target applications. N-doped CDs (N-CDs) are synthesized by ethylene diamine (N source) and sodium lauryl sulphate (favorable for synthesis of smaller N-CDs). The average size of synthesized N-CDs is found in between 2.0 nm to 5.0 nm and they efficaciously have emitted blue FL under UV lamp exposure (∼290-365 nm). Zeta potential shows the net surface charge of N-CDs is negative (−10.4 mV) and plays an important role for the sensitivity of sensor and its binding efficiency towards metal ions. Functional groups (amine, hydroxyl, carbonyl, nitryl and alkenes) present on N-CDs are favorable for quenching and binding probability. Optimum pH was found 4–5 for getting better FL intensity of N-CDs. This FL is disappeared during quenching of certain concentrations of Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions due to the selective quenching of N-CDs with these metal ions. The linear range and optical detection limit of Hg (II) ions are 20–50 μM and 50 nM respectively, while for Fe (III) ions these values are 20–50 μM and 10.0 nM respectively. The fabricated N-CDs are inexpensive colorimetriC–Optical detector, selective and sensitive towards the detection of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions contaminations and could be used as FL sensor probe for the detection of toxic metals sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals/ions are mostly treated as non-biodegradable micro-pollutants and do contaminate/accumulate our biosphere including water and human itself. It is fully accepted that these heavy metals/ions including lead, arsenic, chromium, cadmium, mercury (Hg), copper and iron (Fe) are toxic and hazardous and have an adverse effect to human health and environmental security even at tiny level. The prime sources of these contaminants are effluent of coating, acid battery, alloy, printing, ceramic, glass, pigment and metal plating industries along with herbal products [1–9].

It is believed that Hg (II) is one of the toxic and ubiquitous pollutants among all the existed heavy metal ions due to its non-biodegradability and accumulation in living cells. It may severely affect human by causing kidney, central nervous system, endocrine disorders, different types of irreversible effects (neurological damage, vision and hearing loss), loss in sensational at extremes and birth effects [10,11]. Likewise, Fe (III) is also considered as a critical trace element and omnipresent in the Environment as pollutant [12,13]. Similar to Hg (II), the excess amount of Fe (III) can cause serious human syndromes such as damage internal organs risk, diabetes, cancer and heart attack. Excess availability of Fe (III) in living cell facilitate the oxygenated species production through Fenton reaction which causes damage to lipids, proteins and nucleic acids [14]. Also, it may induce Alzheimers, Parkinson and Huntington disease [15,16]. To conquer these problems, there are several methodologies/techniques are being employed by researcher including atomic absorption spectroscopy, anodic stripping voltammetry, electrochemical methods, coupled plasma mass spectrometry, Rayleigh light scattering, spectrophotometry, gas chromatography–mass spectrometry, selective membrane filtration, X-ray fluorescence spectrometry, fluorescence (FL), and colorimetry to identify these toxic metal/metal ions in biological and environmental samples [2,5,17,18]. Although, most of the methodologies are sensitive and selective but have some drawbacks including time taking process, lengthy sample preparations, expert personnel, expensive and complex [19,20]. Nonetheless, FL and colorimetric analysis are considered more practically feasible due to selectivity, convenient, sensitivity real-time detection and stability towards these toxic metals [21].

There are several FL materials reported in literature including fluorophore and functionally diverse co-linkers into Zn-based structures [1], graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots [2], Hg (II)-ion-imprinted polymer [11], carbon based carbon dots [5,22–24]. Among all, carbon based carbon dots (CDs) are found to be more suitable and appear to be alternate of quantum dot due to its excellent instance stable photoluminescence/FL [25,26], less toxicity [27], outstanding biocompatibility [28] and good solubility in water [29]. The CDs are considered as quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with less than 5 nm size and has been significantly used in wide range of applications because they behave differently concerning size, form and sources of origin. Recently, these CDs are being used as fluorescent probes for sensing of heavy metal ions and selective detection in several fields [30–34]. In addition, it is found that among all existing FL materials these CDs are economical, efficient and environmentally friendly and have been used widely as FL based sensor probes [22,35]. Different approaches have been done for synthesizing CDs including arc-discharge, laser ablation, chemical oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, combustion/ hydrothermal, supported synthesis and microwave heating [23,36].

It is well recognized that doping of heteroatoms nitrogen (N), Sulphur (S), boron (B), phosphorus (P) along with carbon have been enhanced the academic interest and practical applications because heteroatoms characteristically boost the performance of the synthesized materials [22,37,38]. Among all, N doping isresilient and preferred due to the closeness of sizes between nitrogen and carbon atom [3,39–41]. The presence of five-valance electron including two lone pairs of electrons and high electro negativity of N is favorable for the bonding with electro-positive ions preferably trivalent and divalent. These N-CDs may attach to many oxygenated functional groups i.e. acetate, hydroxy and oxy atoms with an irregular system which enhance the efficacy of N-CDs [42,43].

In this context, waste candle soot (CS) derived N-CDs are successfully prepared for detection of Hg (II) and Fe (III) from aqueous medium in this study. These N-CDs are inexpensive with astonishing FL, eco-friendly and have superior water dispersion. As per the author belief, this is the first time when N-CDs are synthesized from waste CS to determine Hg(II) and Fe(III) from water colorimetrically as well as optically. These N-CDs sensor probes are prepared by doping of ethylene diamine and sodium lauryl sulphate (SDS) with CS derived waste carbon nanoparticles (CNPs). The prepared sensor probe is highly selective, low-cost, efficient and sensitive and could be implemented for the detection of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions

Candles used for the preparation of soot were purchased from local market. Ethylene diamine (98% AR grade place) and SDS (99.5% AR grade) were obtained from SRL Pvt. Ltd. India. All metal standards including chromium nitrate, copper nitrate, zinc nitrate, ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3), mercury nitrate (Hg(NO3)2), lead nitrate, calcium nitrate, nickel nitrate, manganese nitrate, cadmium nitrate, and antimony nitrate were procured from Merck, Germany. For this study, Milli-Q water (MQ) was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions. No further purification was done after receiving all reagents.

CNPs were collected in a glass beaker from the CS. CNPs were deposited from the tip of the candle flame until it finished as a previous study [44]. Thereafter, CNPs were treated with four cycles of each 1 M nitric acid and ethyl alcohol for removal of any existed impurity. Next, 150 mg of treated CNPs were dissolved in 3 mL of MQ and sonicated it for 20 min using ultra sonicator to maintain homogeneous dispersion of CNPs in MQ. Prior to sonication, SDS solution (3 mg in 3 mL MQ) was added to CS and MQ mixture. SDS is responsible to enhance the photoresponsivity and external quantum efficiency of N-CDs as well as prevents the agglomeration during the chemical mixing process [45–47]. Thereafter, 15 mL of ethylene diamine was mixed to the solution and vigorously stirred for 6 h at 80 °C with 250-rpm speed. Further, mixture was dried at 150 °C in vacuum oven for 6 h. Afterward; N-CNPs were collected as dark grey glittering crystals. Subsequently, N-CNPs were added in MQ (0.5 mg/mL) and sonicated it for 30 min followed by centrifugation for 20 min at 8000 rpm speed and collected supernatant. The achieved supernatant contained uniformly dispersed N-CDs and treated as stock solution for further analysis. No other treatment was carried out further to N-CDs. The comprehensive steps including fabrication and application of N-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 .

N-CDs morphology and size were examined by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai TM G2 Sprit, FEI, The Netherlands) using 300 size mesh. The broad angle X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Focus, Bruker, Germany) with A Cu-Kα X-ray radiation source (λ = 1.542 A˚) was used for the analysis over a 2θ range of 10–80° and a scan rate of 3° per min. Raman intensity was recorded by Raman spectrometer (Alpha, Witec, Germany) in air at 25 °C over the range of 500-4000 cm−1, using the Ar-ion laser (excitation source, λ = 532 nm) and a CCD detector. The Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were performed to identified surface functional groups using a Tensor 27 apparatus (Bruker, Germany) in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The investigation was done over the wave-number range of 400–4000 cm-1 in attenuated total reflectance mode with a germanium crystal. Sampling was done at 100 scans for the samples, with the resolution of 4 cm−1. During the scanning, the analysis chamber was continuously purged with nitrogen gas to remove atmospheric carbon dioxide and moisture. Net surface charge of the N-CDs suspended in MQ was calculated by Zeta potential using zeta sizer nano series (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments Ltd, United Kingdom). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded with the help of ESCA + omicron nanotechnology oxford instrument. Analysis of spectra was carried out using Casa XPS software and atomic % of each element was calculated with the help of peak area of individual element spectra. UV–vis spectrophotometer (Spectra max M5 series, USA) is used for the absorption analysis of N-CDs. Fluorescence spectra was recorded with Perkin Elmer luminescence spectrometer LS 50B.

Characteristically, 10 μL of stock solution was mixed in 2 mL of 20 mM aqueous solution of various metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Sb3+ and Sn2+) for selectivity and without metal ions with same N-CDs concentration for optical studies. Relative FL intensity of the individual metal ion is calculated with the help of Stern-Volmer equation (
I °
I
= 1 + K s v [ Q ] ) where, I°, I, Ksv and Q were FL intensity of blank (without metal ions), with corresponding metal ions, Stern-Volmer equation constant, and concentration of metal ions respectively. All the FL intensities were measured at a particular excitation wavelength (344 nm, described later) with corresponding metal ion using Spectro- fluorescence meter. The pH study of N-CDs was completed at room temperature (25 ± 5). 0.1 M NaOH and 0.1 M HCl solution were used to maintain pH. However, the 10 μL N-CDs from stock solution were used in 1 mL standard metal ions solution (1 nM-1 μM) for linear range and optical detection limit analysis,

Morphology and size of prepared N-CDs were determined by TEM at low and high-resolution and monographs are presented in Fig. 2 . The shape of all N-CDs were found almost spherical. Size distribution of N-CDs was determined from the Fig. 2a and it varies from 2 nm to 5 nm. Due to its smaller size, N-CDs have revealed remarkable FL and heavy metals particularly Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions have tremendous efficacy towards the quenching of FL. TEM image is also provided after Hg (II) ions quenching in Fig. S1 (Fe (III) ions quenching TEM images are not shown in manuscript due to brevity).

XRD explains (Fig. 2c) structural characterization and lattice phase of prepared N-CDs. There are two distinguish peaks observed: (i) One broad peak centered at ∼25° corresponds to (0 0 2) and (ii) small intensity peak at ∼43° corresponds to (1 0 0) corroborated N-CDs have a mixture of amorphous and graphitized (25°) and graphitic crystalline peaks (43°) respectively. The predominantly broad diffuse peak is typically considered as amorphous structures of carbon but also have little extent of parallel stacked graphene layers while peak at 43° confirms the graphitic structure (crystalline honeycomb lattice carbon) of N-CDs. A typical Raman spectrum of N-CDs is presented in Fig. 2d. Two remarkable sharp peaks have observed at ∼1325 cm−1 and 1545 cm−1.The peaks appeared at 1325 cm−1 and 1545 cm−1 are corroborated to the D band and G band respectively.

The G band ascribes the first order scattering for graphitic character i.e. sp2 hybridized domain while D band observes for disordered graphitic phase. ID/IG of any carbon materials expresses about degree of graphitization. From the data, ID/IG of N-CDs is found ∼0.96, which reveals less disordered carbon in N-CDs. Aforementioned elucidation, confirms the presence of graphitic phase in N-CDs. The graphitic character of the material (N-CDs) is considered as a critical factor for the FL activity of prepared N-CDs, which enhances the photo induced property of the material.

FTIR demonstrates an efficient role to determine the orientation of functional groups present in the surface of prepared N-CDs. Fig. 2e shows the FTIR spectra of CDs (black) and N-CDs (blue). As observed, in CDs five peaks are assigned at 615 cm−1, 1040 cm−1,1620 cm−1, 2350 cm−1 and 3500-3800 cm-1 which may corroborated to CC (alkene stretch), C–O-C (ether stretch), CO2, CO (carbonyl stretch) and OH (hydroxyl stretch). Most of the groups are inherently present in CDs, however the presence of CO2 may be trapped during combustion of candles or from the atmosphere by some group/s present in CDs. Correspondingly, hydroxyl stretch may be due to atmospheric moisture. In addition to peaks observed in CDs, four new peaks are found in N-CDs at 1217-1346 cm−1, 1604 cm−1, 2790-2962 cm−1and 3371 cm−1 which attribute the presence of CN and CNC, NH, CH and NH stretching/bending, respectively. This observation does confirm the doping of nitrogen in N-CDs because these peaks are appeared due to incorporation of ethylene diamine in CDs [48,49]. Net surface charge of the N-CDs is measured by Zeta potential (Fig. S2) and it was found negatively charged (-10.4 mV). These results validate the presence negatively charged functional groups on surface of N-CDs which facilitates the quenching (particularly OH and CO stretch) of N-CDs with Hg(II) and Fe(III) (discussed later in the manuscript) [50]. In addition, the presence of CO stretching of carboxylic acid in N-CDs favorable for water dispersible and generate hydrophilic character.

XPS spectral analysis are carried out to examine the composition of N-CDs and spectra are shown in Fig. 3 . A full scan survey of N-CDs with three distinctive peaks at approximately 284.4 eV, 399.1 eV and 531 eV binding energy are shown in Fig. 3a which corroborate the existence of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s) and oxygen (O1s) respectively, in prepared N-CDs. This also supports the negative surface functionality of N-CDs due to occurrence of these elements as explain earlier in Zeta potential. The total percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are achieved ∼ 77%, 11% and 12% respectively. The high-resolution deconvoluted spectrum of C1S is shown in Fig. 3b which elucidates carbon-binding possibilities in N-CDs. Four distinguish peaks have been observed in spectra at ∼284.4 eV, 285.2 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.1 eV attributed to feature bonding of CC and CC, CN, CO and CO respectively. This also confirms that both amorphous (sp3) and crystalline (sp2) forms are present in N-CDs. Fig. 3c represents the deconvoluted spectrum of N1S. Three major fitted peaks can be assigned at 398.3 eV, 399.4 eV and 400.8 eV due to the bonding of N(C)3, CNC and NH groups respectively. These results confirm the doping of nitrogen in N-CDs. Two deconvoluted binding moieties of O1S are observed in Fig. 3d at 531.7 eV and 533.5 eV assigned to CO and COH respectively. Same observations are also found in literature [51–53].

Different optical characterization techniques were executed to examine the different optical parameter of synthesized N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the maximum absorption is found at 330 nm wavelength during full scan in UV–vis absorption spectrum analysis (10 μL in 2 mL MQ). It is believed that this peak is appeared due to π-π* interaction in aliphatic sp3 hybridization domain and surface groups involvement. When this absorption peak (330 nm) is used as excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission with less intensity is observed at ∼416 nm. Afterward, the excitation and emission bands are optimized 344 nm and 416 nm respectively by changing the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 360 nm (data not shown in the manuscript) using interval of 5 nm in luminescence spectrometer (Fig. 4b).

The significant high FL intensity and strong peak of N-CDs observed at above excitation and emission wavelength. These N-CDs illuminates great intense FL in UV light lamp (265–360 nm) as shown in inset of Fig. 4a. Nonetheless, at this concentration N-CDs were almost colorless in visible light. These FL properties of N-CDs make them predominantly valuable optical sensing probe for sensing selective heavy metal ions. The FL of N-CDs is found stable for more than 60 days (authors have checked only for 60 days). No significant decrease in FL intensity occurs in such a long time. One more observation is made that the amount of prepared N-CDs are required significantly less for substantial high FL from the other reported CDs in literature [1–3,5,11,22,23,34,50,51]. Both of these characteristics may be due to smaller size, nitrogen doping and existed surface functional groups on N-CDs (as explained earlier).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study with different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: This study was carried out to identify the capability of significant detection for heavy metals. N-CDs were added in different heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Sb3+ and Sn2+) of concentration 20 mM and obtained FL spectra within 10 s. The relative FL response of N-CDs after quenching with metal ions with respect to blank N-CDs are shown in Fig. 4c. Among all metal ions, Hg(II) and Fe(III) shows maximum quenching effect with N-CDs and able to quench FL intensity ∼ 46% and 48% respectively. In other words, only Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions show affinity towards N-CDs. One more experiment was performed to check the interferences of other ions mixture along with efficient Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions with same concentration. Quenching result express insignificant interferences of other metal ions. These observations reveal these N-CDs are only sensitive to Hg(II) and Fe(III). It may be attributed that the disappearance of FL after quenching with Hg(II)/Fe(III) due to non-radiative recombination of the excitons by an effective electron transfer process facilitated by closure distance among N-CDs which is accelerated by presence of CO, OH and NH groups. In other words, it could be concluded that FL generated by electron transfer of N-CDs is captured by empty ‘d’ orbital of Hg(II)/Fe(III) ions [50,54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and linearity of Hg (II) and Fe (III)
ce:section.ce:para: The FL spectra of N-CDs are with addition of different concentration of Hg (II) and Fe (III) are shown in Fig. 5 . Different Concentrations i.e. 1μM, 2μM, 3μM, 5μM, 10μM, 20μM, 30μM, 40μM and 50μM, of Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions standard were spiked in N-CDs. As observed from the spectra, FL intensity is condensed monotonously when concentration of Hg(II) and Fe(III) rise. It is noted that the FL of N-CDs are very less when concentration of Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions increases beyond 50 μM as shown in Fig. 5a and b respectively. The reason for quenching already explained in previous section. Nevertheless, there may be one more possibility of disappearance of FL of N-CDs in addition of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions. The electrons from the surface of N-CDs may relocate to these metal ions and leads the formation of ground-state charge transfer complex which absorbs the FL of N-CDs [54]. The linear relationship is also determined between FL of N-CDs and concentrations of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions in the range of 20–50 μM and data points of respective metal ions are shown in inset of Fig. 5a and b. Noteworthy better linear relationships (R2> ∼0.98) are observed for Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions. In addition, optical detections limit is also determined using lower concentration (0–1 μM) of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions. The results of optical detection limit are exposed in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 respectively. The optical detection limit of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions are achieved 50 nM and 10 nM respectively. Fig. S5 shows the disappearance of FL with addition of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions. The optical parameters and efficiency of prepared N-CDs are compared with other optical probes reported in literature and tabulated in Table 1 . The data shows in most of the cases the prepared N-CDs illustrates better response or in few cases, it is competitive with reported probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and solvent with N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability and optical parameter of N-CDs directly depend on pH value of medium. FL intensity changes with pH concentration as shown in Fig. S6. However, the prepared N-CDs shows a significant stability on pH change. But in comparison, when pH is changed from 1 to 4 the FL intensity increased. Afterward FL intensity is gradually decreased as increment of pH. Low FL is observed at pH less than 4 due to increase in protonated nitrogen and oxygen while increase in concentration of deprotonated nitrogen and oxygen at pH more than 6. This may effect excitons distribution resulting generation of low FL [23]. Therefore, pH value plays an important role in FL intensity of N-CDs. On the other hand, solvent used for the dispersion of N-CDs also affects FL intensity of N-CDs. This is because of the variation of polarity of solvent, which will also change the electrons distribution. In N-CDs, the lone pair of nitrogen is participated in the bonding with solvent molecules resulting decrease in FL. Real sample analysis was also performed and discussed in supplementary file (Fig. S7). Fig. S8 shows the FL activity of different solvent medium (e.g. ethanol, benzene, hexane, acetonitrile, acetone and water) with and without N-CDs. The plausible mechanism of FL and quenching with metal ions is already explained in previous sections and schematically represented in Fig. S9.

N-CDs are successfully synthesized in one step using effluent of CS. The process involved in nitrogen doping is very simple and efficient using ethylene diamine resulting ∼ 12% loading of nitrogen on prepared N-CDs. The prepared N-CDs exhibit good water dispersibility, excellent FL, stability, selective and sensitive response towards the identification of Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions. N-CDs show the good linear relationship and significant low optical detection limit for Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions. The overall surface negativity due to nitrogen doping and existed functional groups enhances the optical activity (i.e. FL and quenching effect) of N-CDs. In conclusion, one can say that the overall process to fabricate N-CDs based optical probe is simple, ease, inexpensive and highly effectual for sensing Hg (II) and Fe (III) ions. These N-CDs could be considered as promising, non-toxic and cheap optically active materials for determinations of toxic heavy metals.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi, India for the support in the form of the research grant (DST/INSPIRE/04/2015/001869, GAP 319) to carry out this research work. The authors thank CSIR-Indian Institute of Toxicology Research, Lucknow, for providing necessary infrastructural facilities to perform the present study. CSIR-IITR communication number for this manuscript is 3522.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2018.08.059.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient luminescent metal–Organic framework for the simultaneous detection and removal of heavy metals from water

Carbon dots rooted agarose hydrogel hybrid platform for optical detection and separation of heavy metal ions

Int. J. Hydrogen Energy

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

PLoS One
